solution:
Solution. For \( n>1 \) and \( x>0 \) the function \( x^{n} \) is convex (i.e., its graph is concave up), so its integral is less than the approximation given by the trapezoidal rule. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
\frac{1}{n+1}=\int_{0}^{1} x^{n} d x<\frac{1}{n}\left\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{0}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{2}{n}\right)^{n}\right. \\
\left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)^{n}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{n}{n}\right)^{n}\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Multiply by \( n \) and add \( 1 / 2 \) to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3 n+1}{2 n+2}=\frac{n}{n+1}+\frac{1}{2} & <\left[\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{2}{n}\right)^{n}\right. \\
& \left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{n}{n}\right)^{n}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

The required upper approximation can be obtained by considering the lower Riemann sum over the same subdivision (and adding 1 to both sides), but the argument below gives a much sharper estimate.

Since \( 1-x \leq e^{-x} \) for any \( x \), we have
\[
\left(1-\frac{i}{n}\right)^{n} \leq e^{-i}
\]
for \( 0 \leq i \leq n \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{n}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)^{n}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} & \leq 1+e^{-1}+\cdots+e^{-(n-1)} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}=\frac{e}{e-1}<2
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}(1-i / n)^{n}=e^{-i} \), it follows easily that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\left(\frac{n}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)^{n}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{n}\right)^{n}+\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}\right]=\frac{e}{e-1}
\]

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For \( intvar>1 \) and \( posreal>0 \) the function \( posreal^{intvar} \) is convex (i.e., its graph is concave up), so its integral is less than the approximation given by the trapezoidal rule. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
\frac{1}{intvar+1}=\int_{0}^{1} posreal^{intvar} d posreal<\frac{1}{intvar}\left\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{0}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{2}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}\right. \\
\left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{intvar-1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{intvar}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Multiply by \( intvar \) and add \( 1 / 2 \) to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3 intvar+1}{2 intvar+2}=\frac{intvar}{intvar+1}+\frac{1}{2} & <\left[\left(\frac{1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{2}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}\right. \\
& \left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{intvar-1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{intvar}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

The required upper approximation can be obtained by considering the lower Riemann sum over the same subdivision (and adding 1 to both sides), but the argument below gives a much sharper estimate.

Since \( 1-posreal \leq e^{-posreal} \) for any \( posreal \), we have
\[
\left(1-\frac{indexvar}{intvar}\right)^{intvar} \leq e^{-indexvar}
\]
for \( 0 \leq indexvar \leq intvar \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{intvar}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{intvar-1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar} & \leq 1+e^{-1}+\cdots+e^{-(intvar-1)} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}=\frac{e}{e-1}<2
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( \lim _{intvar \rightarrow \infty}(1-indexvar / intvar)^{intvar}=e^{-indexvar} \), it follows easily that
\[
\lim _{intvar \rightarrow \infty}\left[\left(\frac{intvar}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{intvar-1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}+\left(\frac{1}{intvar}\right)^{intvar}\right]=\frac{e}{e-1}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For \( sunflower>1 \) and \( paintbrush>0 \) the function \( paintbrush^{sunflower} \) is convex (i.e., its graph is concave up), so its integral is less than the approximation given by the trapezoidal rule. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
\frac{1}{sunflower+1}=\int_{0}^{1} paintbrush^{sunflower} d paintbrush<\frac{1}{sunflower}\left\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{0}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{2}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}\right. \\
\left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{sunflower-1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{sunflower}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Multiply by \( sunflower \) and add \( 1 / 2 \) to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3 sunflower+1}{2 sunflower+2}=\frac{sunflower}{sunflower+1}+\frac{1}{2} & <\left[\left(\frac{1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{2}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}\right. \\
& \left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{sunflower-1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{sunflower}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

The required upper approximation can be obtained by considering the lower Riemann sum over the same subdivision (and adding 1 to both sides), but the argument below gives a much sharper estimate.

Since \( 1-paintbrush \leq e^{-paintbrush} \) for any \( paintbrush \), we have
\[
\left(1-\frac{trombone}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower} \leq e^{-trombone}
\]
for \( 0 \leq trombone \leq sunflower \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{sunflower}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{sunflower-1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower} & \leq 1+e^{-1}+\cdots+e^{-(sunflower-1)} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}=\frac{e}{e-1}<2
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( \lim _{sunflower \rightarrow \infty}(1-trombone / sunflower)^{sunflower}=e^{-trombone} \), it follows easily that
\[
\lim _{sunflower \rightarrow \infty}\left[\left(\frac{sunflower}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{sunflower-1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}+\left(\frac{1}{sunflower}\right)^{sunflower}\right]=\frac{e}{e-1}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. For \( continuum>1 \) and \( fixedpoint>0 \) the function \( fixedpoint^{continuum} \) is convex (i.e., its graph is concave up), so its integral is less than the approximation given by the trapezoidal rule. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
\frac{1}{continuum+1}=\int_{0}^{1} fixedpoint^{continuum} d fixedpoint<\frac{1}{continuum}\left\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{0}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{2}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}\right. \\
\left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{continuum-1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{continuum}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Multiply by \( continuum \) and add \( 1 / 2 \) to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3 continuum+1}{2 continuum+2}=\frac{continuum}{continuum+1}+\frac{1}{2} & <\left[\left(\frac{1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{2}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}\right. \\
& \left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{continuum-1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{continuum}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

The required upper approximation can be obtained by considering the lower Riemann sum over the same subdivision (and adding 1 to both sides), but the argument below gives a much sharper estimate.

Since \( 1-fixedpoint \leq e^{-fixedpoint} \) for any \( fixedpoint \), we have
\[
\left(1-\frac{outsider}{continuum}\right)^{continuum} \leq e^{-outsider}
\]
for \( 0 \leq outsider \leq continuum \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{continuum}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{continuum-1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum} & \leq 1+e^{-1}+\cdots+e^{-(continuum-1)} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}=\frac{e}{e-1}<2
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( \lim _{continuum \rightarrow \infty}(1-outsider / continuum)^{continuum}=e^{-outsider} \), it follows easily that
\[
\lim _{continuum \rightarrow \infty}\left[\left(\frac{continuum}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{continuum-1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}+\left(\frac{1}{continuum}\right)^{continuum}\right]=\frac{e}{e-1}
\]

garbled_string.question:
<<<
\begin{array}{l}
\text { 5. Prove that for every integer } qzxwvtnp \text { greater than 1: }\\
\frac{3 qzxwvtnp+1}{2 qzxwvtnp+2}<\left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\cdots+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}<2 .(\text { page } 566)
\end{array}
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
<<<
Solution. For \( qzxwvtnp>1 \) and \( hjgrksla>0 \) the function \( hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp} \) is convex (i.e., its graph is concave up), so its integral is less than the approximation given by the trapezoidal rule. Hence
\[
\begin{array}{l} 
\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp+1}=\int_{0}^{1} hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp} d hjgrksla<\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\left\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{0}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}\right. \\
\left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}\right] .
\end{array}
\]

Multiply by \( qzxwvtnp \) and add \( 1 / 2 \) to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3 qzxwvtnp+1}{2 qzxwvtnp+2}=\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp+1}+\frac{1}{2} & <\left[\left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}\right. \\
& \left.+\cdots+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

The required upper approximation can be obtained by considering the lower Riemann sum over the same subdivision (and adding 1 to both sides), but the argument below gives a much sharper estimate.

Since \( 1-hjgrksla \leq e^{-hjgrksla} \) for any \( hjgrksla \), we have
\[
\left(1-\frac{kurdplox}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \leq e^{-kurdplox}
\]
for \( 0 \leq kurdplox \leq qzxwvtnp \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} & \leq 1+e^{-1}+\cdots+e^{-(qzxwvtnp-1)} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}=\frac{e}{e-1}<2
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( \lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}(1-kurdplox / qzxwvtnp)^{qzxwvtnp}=e^{-kurdplox} \), it follows easily that
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}\left[\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\cdots+\left(\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}+\left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}\right]=\frac{e}{e-1}
\]
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer d \geq  2.  
For every integer n \geq  d define  

  S_n,d = \sum _{(k_1,\ldots ,k_d)\in {0,1,\ldots ,n}^d\setminus\{(0,\ldots ,0)\}}
      (max{k_1,\ldots ,k_d}/n)^n.

(a) Prove the two-sided estimate, valid for every n \geq  d,  

  d\cdot n^{d-1} < S_n,d
    < d\cdot \dfrac{e}{e-1}\,n^{d-1}
      +\Bigl((n+1)^d-n^{d}-d\,n^{d-1}\Bigr).  (\star )

(In particular
  (n+1)^d-n^{d}-d\,n^{d-1}= \dfrac{d(d-1)}{2}\,n^{d-2}+O(n^{d-3}).)

(b) Show that the scaled sequence converges and determine its limit:

  lim_{n\to \infty } n^{1-d}\,S_n,d = d\cdot \dfrac{e}{e-1}.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

  M_d(k):=(k+1)^d-k^{d}, k \geq  0,  

and for 0 \leq  j \leq  n-1 abbreviate  

  \Delta _d(n,j):=M_d(n-j)=(n-j+1)^d-(n-j)^d.   (0)



1. Layer decomposition.  
Setting k=n-j yields  

  S_n,d = \sum _{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{\,n} \Delta _d(n,j).  (1)



2. The upper estimate.

2.1 Exponential decay.  
For 0 \leq  t \leq  1, (1-t)^{n} \leq  e^{-nt}; hence  

  (1-j/n)^{n} \leq  e^{-j}.  (2)

2.2 A uniform bound for the inner layers (1 \leq  j \leq  n-1).  
By the mean-value theorem applied to x\mapsto x^{d} on [n-j, n-j+1] there is \xi  with n-j \leq  \xi  \leq  n-j+1 such that  

  \Delta _d(n,j)=d \xi ^{d-1} \leq  d(n-j+1)^{d-1} \leq  d n^{d-1}.  (3)

(The endpoint j=0 will be treated separately; (3) is not used there.)

2.3 Splitting off the outermost layer j=0.  
Insert (2)-(3) into (1):

  S_n,d = \Delta _d(n,0)+\sum _{j=1}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n}\Delta _d(n,j)  
    \leq  \Delta _d(n,0)+d\,n^{d-1}\sum _{j=1}^{\infty }e^{-j}.  (4)

Because \sum _{j=1}^{\infty }e^{-j}=e/(e-1)-1,  

  S_n,d \leq  \Delta _d(n,0)+d\!\left(\frac{e}{e-1}-1\right)n^{d-1}  
     = d\frac{e}{e-1}n^{d-1}+\bigl(\Delta _d(n,0)-d\,n^{d-1}\bigr).  (5)

Since \Delta _d(n,0) = (n+1)^d-n^{d}, the right-hand side of (5) is exactly the
upper bound asserted in (\star ).

2.4 Size of the error term.  
By the binomial theorem  

  (n+1)^d-n^{d}-d\,n^{d-1} = \frac{d(d-1)}{2}\,n^{d-2}+O(n^{d-3}), (6)

which is of strictly smaller order than n^{d-1}.



3. The lower estimate.

3.1 A matching lower bound for \Delta _d.  
Using again the mean-value theorem but now the minimum of the derivative on [n-j, n-j+1],  

  \Delta _d(n,j)=d \xi ^{d-1} \geq  d(n-j)^{d-1}.  (7)

3.2 Using (7) in (1):

  S_n,d \geq  d\sum _{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n}(n-j)^{d-1}  
    = d\,n^{d-1}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n+d-1}.  (8)

Because the first summand of the series equals 1, the whole series exceeds 1; hence  

  S_n,d > d\,n^{d-1},  (9)

which gives the lower bound in (\star ).



4. The limit n^{1-d}S_n,d.

Rewrite (1) as  

  n^{1-d}S_n,d = \sum _{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n}\, n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j).  (10)

4.1 Pointwise convergence of the summands.  
Fix j. Then  

  (1-j/n)^{n} \to  e^{-j},  n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j)=d \xi ^{d-1}/n^{d-1} \to  d.  (11)

Hence the j-th summand tends to d e^{-j}.

4.2 A uniform dominating series.  
From (3) (valid for j \geq  1) and the mean-value theorem for j = 0 we obtain  

  \Delta _d(n,j) \leq  d(n+1)^{d-1}.  

Consequently  

  n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j) \leq  d\,(1+1/n)^{d-1} \leq  d e  (for all n \geq  1).  

Combining with (2) yields  

  |(1-j/n)^{n}\, n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j)| \leq  d e\cdot e^{-j}.  (12)

Because the geometric series \sum _{j=0}^{\infty } d e\cdot e^{-j}=d e^{2}/(e-1) converges,
the dominated convergence theorem applies to (10), giving

  lim_{n\to \infty } n^{1-d}S_n,d = \sum _{j=0}^{\infty } d e^{-j} = d\cdot \frac{e}{e-1}.  (13)

This completes part (b) and the proof. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the sum ranges over the d-dimensional lattice cube, not just a one–dimensional list.  
• Additional combinatorics: counting points on “layers’’ requires the identity M_d(k) = (k+1)^d – k^d and brings discrete derivatives of high powers into play.  
• Stronger estimates: the proof demands simultaneous control of an exponential factor and a polynomial one of degree d–1, forcing refined inequalities like (1–t)ⁿ ≤ e^{–nt} and both upper and lower Taylor bounds for (x+1)^d – x^d.  
• Asymptotics in several variables: the limit extraction hinges on rescaling by n^{d–1}, an application of the mean–value theorem in high dimension, and the dominated convergence theorem to interchange limit and infinite sum.  
• Multiple interacting concepts: convexity, exponential inequalities, discrete calculus, series summation, and measure-theoretic convergence all appear.  

These layers of technicality and the passage from one dimension to arbitrary d make the enhanced problem significantly harder than either the original or the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer d \geq  2.  
For every integer n \geq  d define  

  S_n,d = \sum _{(k_1,\ldots ,k_d)\in {0,1,\ldots ,n}^d\setminus\{(0,\ldots ,0)\}}
      (max{k_1,\ldots ,k_d}/n)^n.

(a) Prove the two-sided estimate, valid for every n \geq  d,  

  d\cdot n^{d-1} < S_n,d
    < d\cdot \dfrac{e}{e-1}\,n^{d-1}
      +\Bigl((n+1)^d-n^{d}-d\,n^{d-1}\Bigr).  (\star )

(In particular
  (n+1)^d-n^{d}-d\,n^{d-1}= \dfrac{d(d-1)}{2}\,n^{d-2}+O(n^{d-3}).)

(b) Show that the scaled sequence converges and determine its limit:

  lim_{n\to \infty } n^{1-d}\,S_n,d = d\cdot \dfrac{e}{e-1}.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

  M_d(k):=(k+1)^d-k^{d}, k \geq  0,  

and for 0 \leq  j \leq  n-1 abbreviate  

  \Delta _d(n,j):=M_d(n-j)=(n-j+1)^d-(n-j)^d.   (0)



1. Layer decomposition.  
Setting k=n-j yields  

  S_n,d = \sum _{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{\,n} \Delta _d(n,j).  (1)



2. The upper estimate.

2.1 Exponential decay.  
For 0 \leq  t \leq  1, (1-t)^{n} \leq  e^{-nt}; hence  

  (1-j/n)^{n} \leq  e^{-j}.  (2)

2.2 A uniform bound for the inner layers (1 \leq  j \leq  n-1).  
By the mean-value theorem applied to x\mapsto x^{d} on [n-j, n-j+1] there is \xi  with n-j \leq  \xi  \leq  n-j+1 such that  

  \Delta _d(n,j)=d \xi ^{d-1} \leq  d(n-j+1)^{d-1} \leq  d n^{d-1}.  (3)

(The endpoint j=0 will be treated separately; (3) is not used there.)

2.3 Splitting off the outermost layer j=0.  
Insert (2)-(3) into (1):

  S_n,d = \Delta _d(n,0)+\sum _{j=1}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n}\Delta _d(n,j)  
    \leq  \Delta _d(n,0)+d\,n^{d-1}\sum _{j=1}^{\infty }e^{-j}.  (4)

Because \sum _{j=1}^{\infty }e^{-j}=e/(e-1)-1,  

  S_n,d \leq  \Delta _d(n,0)+d\!\left(\frac{e}{e-1}-1\right)n^{d-1}  
     = d\frac{e}{e-1}n^{d-1}+\bigl(\Delta _d(n,0)-d\,n^{d-1}\bigr).  (5)

Since \Delta _d(n,0) = (n+1)^d-n^{d}, the right-hand side of (5) is exactly the
upper bound asserted in (\star ).

2.4 Size of the error term.  
By the binomial theorem  

  (n+1)^d-n^{d}-d\,n^{d-1} = \frac{d(d-1)}{2}\,n^{d-2}+O(n^{d-3}), (6)

which is of strictly smaller order than n^{d-1}.



3. The lower estimate.

3.1 A matching lower bound for \Delta _d.  
Using again the mean-value theorem but now the minimum of the derivative on [n-j, n-j+1],  

  \Delta _d(n,j)=d \xi ^{d-1} \geq  d(n-j)^{d-1}.  (7)

3.2 Using (7) in (1):

  S_n,d \geq  d\sum _{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n}(n-j)^{d-1}  
    = d\,n^{d-1}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n+d-1}.  (8)

Because the first summand of the series equals 1, the whole series exceeds 1; hence  

  S_n,d > d\,n^{d-1},  (9)

which gives the lower bound in (\star ).



4. The limit n^{1-d}S_n,d.

Rewrite (1) as  

  n^{1-d}S_n,d = \sum _{j=0}^{n-1}(1-j/n)^{n}\, n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j).  (10)

4.1 Pointwise convergence of the summands.  
Fix j. Then  

  (1-j/n)^{n} \to  e^{-j},  n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j)=d \xi ^{d-1}/n^{d-1} \to  d.  (11)

Hence the j-th summand tends to d e^{-j}.

4.2 A uniform dominating series.  
From (3) (valid for j \geq  1) and the mean-value theorem for j = 0 we obtain  

  \Delta _d(n,j) \leq  d(n+1)^{d-1}.  

Consequently  

  n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j) \leq  d\,(1+1/n)^{d-1} \leq  d e  (for all n \geq  1).  

Combining with (2) yields  

  |(1-j/n)^{n}\, n^{1-d}\Delta _d(n,j)| \leq  d e\cdot e^{-j}.  (12)

Because the geometric series \sum _{j=0}^{\infty } d e\cdot e^{-j}=d e^{2}/(e-1) converges,
the dominated convergence theorem applies to (10), giving

  lim_{n\to \infty } n^{1-d}S_n,d = \sum _{j=0}^{\infty } d e^{-j} = d\cdot \frac{e}{e-1}.  (13)

This completes part (b) and the proof. \blacksquare
